Rose Madder
nytimes_articles.abstract: ose Madder," the title of Stephen King's 29th novel, refers of course to the reddish pigment used in painting, on which depend important details of the novel's scary plot. But "Rose Madder" also sums up what the novel's heroine feels when she notices "a single drop of blood, no larger than a dime" on the sheet near her pillow while she is making the bed.

Metro Digest
Metropolitan Diary
Adolph S. Ochs of The Times, in the First Person Singular
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In August 1896, the head of a major New York life insurance company gave some advice to a young newspaperman from Chattanooga, Tenn.: "Don't do yourself anything you can get others to do as well. Confine your efforts to the impossible things." The younger man, Adolph Simon Ochs, was making a career of doing things other people thought impossible. At age 20, in 1878, he had bought the failing Chattanooga Times with borrowed money -- his father had had to sign the papers since Adolph was legally underage -- and within a decade had  turned it into one of the most successful newspapers in the South. And in August 1896, the 38-year-old greenhorn was about to acquire a controlling interest in what he called "one of the greatest newspapers in the world": The New York Times. 

     An exhibition opening today at the Pierpont Morgan Library, "Documenting The  Times: Adolph S. Ochs and the Early Years of The New York Times," tells this extraordinary story through letters, manuscripts, photographs, and cartoons from private Times archives. Artfully organized by the Morgan's associate curator of  autograph manuscripts, Christine Nelson, the materials frame the events of 1896. There are thoughts by de Tocqueville and Thoreau on America's romance with the news, and a photograph of the first Times building, on Printing House Square. Only once, in 1883, did The Times use color on its front page, shown here, a stripe down one side announcing that the price would be cut. The paper hyphenated "New-York" on its nameplate until Ochs changed the adjective to a statement at the end of 1896.

Enigma of the Grill: To Cover or Not
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE best grilled food is -- well -- grilled. It is cooked directly over the heat and flame of a live fire. Reach for the cover and you are cooking by another method -- which means you are likely to end up eating food with a flavor that you never expected. 

     The flavor of the cover.

Before the Testimony Began, the Verdict Was In
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The hearings of the Senate Whitewater committee illustrated how the lack of legal protections allow politicians to misuse witnesses and distort testimony. The result of such bias: two final reports divided along partisan lines. 

     The panel's recently completed hearings resembled a judicial proceeding because witnesses testified in response to questions from senators. But the hearings differed in one important way: There was no impartial arbiter to rule on disputes or to prevent senators from improper questioning.

SOCCER;Germans Defeat English In Shootout
nytimes_articles.abstract:
England perished in the semifinals of the European soccer championships the same way it had triumphed in the quarterfinals, beaten by Germany's greater mastery of the penalty shootout. 

     Since Germany lost the 1976 final on penalties to Czechoslovakia, it had been in three penalty shootouts, all in the World Cup, and won them all. Tonight, it  repeated its triumph over England in Turin, Italy, six years ago to reach the finals where it will face -- how these patterns recur -- the Czech Republic.

Keeping Saudi Arabia in Perspective
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The simplest explanation for Tuesday night's bombing in Saudi Arabia, which killed 19 Americans, may turn out to be the correct one. We should not be surprised if the attack was carried out by people from the same indigenous group of militants responsible for the bombing of a National Guard building in Riyadh last November. 

     Three of the four bombers in that attack stated in their televised confessions that they had fought for Islamic militant groups in Afghanistan, where they had been trained in the use of explosives. Many men like them have returned home violent, unemployable and imbued with anti-Western ideas that make them want to purge their nation of what they see as America's corrupting influence.
